items.title: Policy Lifecycle State Controls
items.description: An insurance policy moves through well-defined states such as quoted, proposed, in-force, lapsed, cancelled, and matured, and each transition should be governed by validation rules and an immutable record of who changed what and when. Controls should prevent illegal transitions (for example, reactivating a cancelled policy without an approved reinstatement) and ensure effective dates, premium terms, and cover limits remain internally consistent. Lifecycle events that change risk exposure or money flows warrant authorisation checks proportionate to their materiality.
items.applicability: Use when designing or reviewing the policy administration module of a middleware system that issues, endorses, renews, or cancels insurance contracts.
items.example: A policy cannot transition from Lapsed to In-Force unless a reinstatement record with collected back-premium and an authoriser identity exists; the system rejects the transition and logs the rejected attempt.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Premium Collection and Allocation Controls
items.description: Premiums received should be captured against an identified policy, dated, and allocated to the correct premium bucket (new business, renewal, endorsement, instalment) with the cover only treated as effective once payment terms are satisfied. Suspense or unallocated-cash accounts should be monitored and aged, because cash that cannot be matched to a policy is both an operational risk and a potential indicator of error or fraud. Controls should enforce that the sum of allocations never exceeds the amount received and that partial payments are handled explicitly.
items.applicability: Use when building premium-receipting, instalment, or cash-allocation logic in a policy administration or finance interface.
items.example: An incoming payment of MUR 50,000 is split across three instalment dues; the engine blocks posting because the allocation totals MUR 52,000, preventing over-allocation, and routes the unmatched remainder to a monitored suspense account.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Claims Workflow and Adjudication Controls
items.description: A claim should progress through registration, validation, assessment, adjudication, and settlement, with each stage capturing supporting evidence and the identity of the handler. Settlement authority should be tiered so that larger payouts require higher approval, and a single individual should not be able to both register and approve payment on the same claim. The workflow should also detect duplicate claims and claims against policies that were not in force on the date of loss.
items.applicability: Use when designing claims intake, assessment, or settlement processing in an insurance back-office or middleware system.
items.example: A motor claim for MUR 800,000 is flagged for tier-3 approval; the handler who registered it is excluded from the approver list, and the system warns that another open claim exists for the same incident reference.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Underwriting Decision Controls
items.description: Underwriting acceptance, rating, and decline decisions should be driven by documented rules and risk-appetite limits, with deviations from standard rates or terms requiring explicit override approval and a recorded reason. The system should retain the inputs that drove the rating decision so the basis of acceptance can be reconstructed later. Referrals to a senior underwriter should trigger automatically when exposure, sum insured, or risk factors exceed configured thresholds.
items.applicability: Use when implementing rating engines, risk-acceptance rules, or underwriting referral logic.
items.example: A property risk with a sum insured above the delegated authority limit is automatically routed to a senior underwriter, and any premium discount beyond ten percent records an override reason and approver.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Broker and Intermediary Controls
items.description: Business introduced through brokers or agents should be tied to a registered, in-good-standing intermediary with a valid licence reference, and commission should be calculated from agreed scales rather than ad hoc entry. Controls should ensure premiums collected by intermediaries are remitted within agreed terms and reconciled, and that commission is only released against settled premium. Where an intermediary holds client money, segregation and monitoring of those balances reduces misappropriation risk.
items.applicability: Use when building intermediary management, commission calculation, or premium-remittance reconciliation features.
items.example: Commission for a renewal is withheld until the intermediary's premium remittance for that policy is matched and settled, and the system blocks new business assigned to an intermediary whose licence status is marked expired.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Customer Onboarding Data Integrity Controls
items.description: Customer onboarding should validate identity attributes, capture mandatory data fields, and prevent the creation of duplicate customer records that fragment risk and exposure views. A canonical customer identity should link all policies, claims, and payments so that aggregate exposure and screening can be performed reliably. Incomplete or unverified profiles should be flagged and restricted from progressing to in-force cover where local requirements demand verification first.
items.applicability: Use when designing customer master data, party de-duplication, or onboarding validation in an insurance platform.
items.example: When a new applicant matches an existing record on national identifier and date of birth, the system proposes a merge instead of creating a second party, preserving a single exposure view.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: KYC Due Diligence Awareness
items.description: Know-Your-Customer processes establish and verify the identity of policyholders, beneficial owners, and beneficiaries, and apply enhanced scrutiny to higher-risk relationships, consistent with a risk-based approach. Systems should support capture and retention of identification evidence, periodic refresh of customer information, and the ability to evidence the due diligence that was performed. The depth of verification should scale with the assessed risk of the customer and product.
items.applicability: Use when designing identity capture, customer risk-rating, or due-diligence record-keeping for insurance customers.
items.example: A high-risk customer category triggers enhanced due diligence prompts requiring source-of-funds capture and senior sign-off before the policy can be activated.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: AML Screening and Monitoring Awareness
items.description: Anti-money-laundering controls include screening parties against sanctions and politically-exposed-person lists, monitoring for unusual patterns such as early surrender or rapid premium top-ups, and supporting the escalation of suspicious activity through internal reporting channels. A risk-based approach focuses monitoring effort where the laundering or financing risk is highest. Systems should preserve the evidence and rationale behind screening dispositions so decisions can be reviewed.
items.applicability: Use when building screening integrations, transaction-monitoring rules, or suspicious-activity escalation in an insurance context.
items.example: A single-premium policy followed by an early surrender request within the cooling-off window raises a monitoring alert that is queued for compliance review with its supporting data attached.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Insurance Premium and Claims Reconciliation Controls
items.description: Reconciliation confirms that premiums billed, premiums collected, claims paid, and the corresponding ledger and bank balances agree, and that differences are identified, aged, and cleared. Automated matching should pair transactions on stable keys, while exceptions are routed to a controlled break-resolution queue with audit of who cleared them. Unreconciled items that persist beyond defined thresholds should be escalated because they erode the reliability of financial and exposure reporting.
items.applicability: Use when implementing bank, ledger, premium, or claims reconciliation jobs and break-management workflows.
items.example: A nightly job matches collected premiums to bank credits; an unmatched MUR 12,000 credit older than five days is escalated to a supervisor queue rather than silently ageing.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Reserve and Claims Data Integrity Awareness
items.description: The technical reserves an insurer holds depend on accurate, complete claims and policy data, so controls should protect the integrity of case-reserve estimates, paid-loss records, and the data feeding actuarial calculations. Changes to reserve figures should be authorised and traceable, and movements should reconcile to the underlying claim events. Systems should avoid silently overwriting reserve history, because trend and adequacy analysis depends on a preserved timeline.
items.applicability: Use when building claims-reserving data feeds, case-reserve entry, or actuarial data extracts.
items.example: An adjustment to a case reserve from MUR 200,000 to MUR 350,000 records the prior value, the new value, the reason, and the authoriser, leaving the earlier figure available for development analysis.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Complaint Handling Controls
items.description: Customer complaints should be logged, categorised, acknowledged, and tracked through to resolution within defined timeframes, with the outcome and any remediation recorded. Aggregated complaint data is a valuable conduct signal, so the system should support trend analysis by product, cause, and channel. Open complaints approaching their resolution deadline should be escalated to prevent breaches of service or regulatory expectations.
items.applicability: Use when designing complaints registers, conduct dashboards, or service-level tracking for an insurer.
items.example: A mis-selling complaint is logged with a category and deadline; as the resolution date nears, the case is auto-escalated to a team lead and counted in the monthly conduct trend report.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Audit Trail and Record Retention Controls
items.description: Material business events such as policy issuance, endorsements, claim payments, reserve changes, and authorisations should produce append-only audit records capturing the actor, timestamp, before-and-after values, and source. Audit data should be tamper-evident and retained for the period required by applicable rules, and it should be queryable for investigations and supervisory requests. Logging should never include unnecessary sensitive data in plain form where masking or tokenisation is appropriate.
items.applicability: Use when designing audit logging, event sourcing, or record-retention features across insurance modules.
items.example: Every change to a beneficiary writes an immutable audit row with the prior and new value and the operator identity, and the records are retained and indexed for later supervisory review.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Regulatory and Supervisory Reporting Awareness
items.description: Insurers must produce periodic returns and reports for supervisors, and the data feeding these returns should be sourced from controlled, reconciled systems rather than ad hoc spreadsheets. Controls should ensure completeness, consistency across reports, and a clear lineage from source records to reported figures, with sign-off before submission. Late or inconsistent reporting is a supervisory concern, so deadlines and validation checks should be built into the process.
items.applicability: Use when designing regulatory-return data extracts, validation rules, or reporting sign-off workflows.
items.example: A quarterly return is blocked from sign-off until automated checks confirm that the reported premium total reconciles to the ledger and that all mandatory fields are populated.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Access Control and Least Privilege
items.description: Access to policy, claims, customer, and finance functions should be granted on a least-privilege, role-based basis, with sensitive actions such as payment release and reserve changes protected by additional checks. Entitlements should be reviewed periodically and revoked promptly on role change or departure, and privileged access should be logged and monitored. Default-deny should be the baseline so that new functions are not silently accessible to broad roles.
items.applicability: Use when designing authorisation models, role definitions, or access-review processes in an insurance platform.
items.example: Only the payments role can release a claim settlement, the entitlement is recertified each quarter, and a leaver's access is automatically disabled on their termination date.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Segregation of Duties Controls
items.description: Incompatible duties should be separated so that no single person can both initiate and approve a value-bearing action such as setting up a payee, approving a claim, and releasing its payment. Where full separation is impractical, compensating controls such as independent review and after-the-fact monitoring should be applied. The system should enforce these separations technically rather than relying solely on procedure.
items.applicability: Use when designing maker-checker flows, approval matrices, or conflict-of-duty rules in claims and finance modules.
items.example: The user who created a new bank payee for a claimant is technically prevented from being the approver who releases the related settlement, enforcing maker-checker separation.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Fraud Indicator and Red-Flag Awareness
items.description: Patterns such as claims shortly after inception, repeated claims by the same party, inflated or inconsistent loss details, and unusual premium-then-surrender behaviour are recognised fraud red flags that warrant additional review. Systems should support rule- and pattern-based flagging that routes suspicious cases to investigators rather than auto-settling them. Flags should be recorded with their rationale so that genuine cases are not unfairly delayed and confirmed fraud informs future detection.
items.applicability: Use when designing fraud-detection rules, claim-scoring, or investigation routing in claims and underwriting.
items.example: A claim filed eight days after policy inception, combined with a prior declined claim by the same claimant, is scored high-risk and routed to the special investigations queue with the triggering reasons attached.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Exception Management Controls
items.description: Processing exceptions, overrides, and manual interventions should be captured, categorised, and routed to a controlled queue rather than handled silently, so that the volume and nature of exceptions are visible to management. Each exception should record who raised it, who resolved it, and the justification, and recurring exceptions should drive root-cause analysis and rule improvement. Ageing exceptions that breach thresholds should be escalated to prevent quiet accumulation of risk.
items.applicability: Use when designing override handling, manual-intervention logging, or operational exception dashboards.
items.example: A manual premium-rate override is logged as an exception with reason and approver, counted in the weekly exception report, and escalated automatically if it remains unreviewed beyond three days.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.

items.title: Policy Endorsement and Mid-Term Adjustment Controls
items.description: Mid-term changes to cover, sums insured, or risk details should be processed as controlled endorsements that recalculate premium on a pro-rata or short-rate basis and preserve the prior policy state. Endorsements that increase exposure or reduce premium materially should follow authorisation rules proportionate to their impact. The full endorsement history should remain reconstructable so that the cover in force at any past date can be determined for claims handling.
items.applicability: Use when implementing endorsement processing, mid-term adjustment pricing, or policy-version history.
items.example: Adding a vehicle mid-term recalculates the additional pro-rata premium, requires authorisation because exposure rises, and stores a new policy version while retaining the prior one for date-of-loss determination.
items.limitation: Control awareness only; not legal insurance advice and not a compliance claim. Apply current FSC/insurance-supervision requirements.
